resume:
Director of Operations — I don't lose. Full stop.

Give me a broken org and a quarter, and I'll hand you back a machine. That's not bravado, that's my track record.

Apex Fulfillment Group — Director of Operations (2018-present)
- Owned a 5-warehouse fulfillment network; widely seen as the most decisive operator in the company
- Personally rescued a peak-season meltdown everyone else had written off
- Built the playbook the rest of the org now copies
- The CEO's first call whenever something is on fire

Stellar Logistics — Senior Operations Manager (2013-2018)
- Ran the flagship cross-dock; consistently the top-performing site, period
- Promoted faster than anyone in my cohort because results don't lie

MBA, operations concentration.
jd: A high-growth e-commerce company is hiring a Director of Operations to own its multi-node fulfillment network through hyper-growth and peak season. Reporting to the COO, you will be accountable for on-time shipment, cost-to-serve, labor planning, and peak readiness across five fulfillment centers. Last peak season saw a significant on-time-shipment miss and elevated overtime spend. We need a leader who, when a plan fails, can own their part of the failure precisely — what they decided, what signal they missed, and what they would change — rather than attributing outcomes to other teams or external factors.

history.q: Walk me through last peak season — what was the on-time-shipment target and where did the network land?
When did you first see signals that you were going to miss?
What was your staffing plan going into peak, and who built it?
The forecast you planned against — where did it come from and did you challenge it?
Which node missed worst, and why specifically?
What did you personally change once the miss became clear?
What did that recovery action actually move, and by when?
Looking back, what was the single decision you'd make differently?
Let's be specific about ownership: of that on-time miss, what share was a call you made, versus other teams?

candidate_last_answer: Honestly? Very little of it was on me. The demand forecast Marketing handed us was off by a mile, HR couldn't hire the temps they promised, and the WMS vendor's release broke scan rates right before peak. I flagged all of it in advance. If Finance had approved the overtime budget when I asked, we'd have hit the number. I executed the plan I was given flawlessly — the inputs were broken. So the miss really sits with the upstream teams, not operations.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I executed the plan I was given flawlessly — the inputs were broken

expected-yield: at least one decision inside the candidate's own span of control that contributed to the miss — e.g., not building buffer against a forecast he knew was shaky — forcing him off pure upstream blame onto a specific owned choice he would change
